metadata.summary: Design liveness and readiness health-check endpoints for APIs: separate concerns, return correct status codes, and avoid cascade failures under k8s.

content.title: API Health Check Endpoints: Liveness vs. Readiness
content.summary: Every API must expose two distinct health endpoints: /healthz (liveness — process alive, no downstream calls) and /readyz (readiness — all dependencies reachable). Conflating the two causes cascade restart loops under Kubernetes.

why.p: Kubernetes (and most orchestrators) use separate probes for different failure modes. Liveness probes trigger container restarts; if a liveness probe calls downstream (DB, Redis, Stripe) and those dependencies are slow, every service restarts in a cascade. Readiness probes gate traffic routing; they should check all dependencies but never trigger restarts. Getting this wrong causes cascading failure at scale.

A second failure mode is false liveness: a /health endpoint that just returns 200 provides no signal about degraded dependencies, causing traffic to route to broken instances. The readiness probe catches this.

list.item: Any API service deployed under Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, ECS, or any orchestrator that uses liveness/readiness distinctions.
When adding observability to a new Python/Node/Go/Java API for the first time.
Before a planned launch or scale event — health probes must exist before load balancers are configured.
When migrating from a single /health endpoint to a split liveness/readiness pattern.
Pre-product-fit prototypes — /health returning 200 with log-to-stdout is enough; full probe separation adds overhead without value.
Static sites and CDN-only deployments — the CDN provider's health checks already cover availability.
One-shot batch jobs and cron tasks — use job-completion alerts (Healthchecks.io, Dead Man's Snitch) instead.

rule.statement: Liveness endpoint (/healthz) MUST check ONLY that the process is alive. It MUST NOT call any downstream service (database, Redis, external API). It returns 200 with a JSON body containing status and timestamp.
rule.rationale: If liveness calls downstream and that downstream is slow, Kubernetes restarts the healthy process. This turns a dependency outage into a cascading restart loop that takes down your service even though the process itself was fine.

rule.statement: Readiness endpoint (/readyz) MUST check all critical dependencies (database, cache, external APIs). It MUST return 503 with the failing check names in the response body when any dependency is unreachable. It MUST never trigger a container restart — wire to readiness probes only, never liveness.
rule.rationale: Readiness gates traffic. When readiness fails, the orchestrator removes the pod from load-balancer rotation; the process stays running and can recover when the dependency comes back.

rule.statement: External dependency URLs in readiness probes MUST be from the vendor's documented health/status contract (e.g., Stripe: https://status.stripe.com/api/v2/status.json) or an in-house canary endpoint. Invented URLs like https://api.stripe.com/health are fictional; the probe will always fail.
rule.rationale: AI agents and copy-paste culture produce hallucinated vendor health URLs constantly. Agents frequently use README's https://api.stripe.com/health — that URL does not exist. Always verify against vendor docs before wiring.

example.title: Split liveness and readiness in FastAPI
example.note: Liveness calls nothing downstream. Readiness checks DB + Redis + external and returns 503 on failure. Stripe uses the real documented status API, not a fictional /health URL.

example.title: Single /health endpoint used as liveness probe
example.code: # DO NOT DO THIS — liveness probe calls downstream @app.get("/health") async def health_check(): await db.execute("SELECT 1") # downstream call in liveness await redis.ping() # downstream call in liveness r = await client.get("https://api.stripe.com/health", timeout=5) # fictional URL return {"status": "ok"}
example.note: This causes a cascade restart loop if DB or Redis is slow. The Stripe URL is fictional and will always fail. Using this as a Kubernetes liveness probe brings down a healthy service when its dependencies are degraded.

section.name: key-metrics-targets
section.p: Target thresholds for API health monitoring:

list.item: Response time p50: below 100ms | Alert threshold: above 200ms
Response time p95: below 500ms | Alert threshold: above 1s
Response time p99: below 1s | Alert threshold: above 2s
Error rate target: below 0.1% | Alert threshold: above 1%
Rate limit hits: low baseline | Alert on sudden spike

checklist.check: Define SLOs (service level objectives) before wiring any probes.
Verify database connectivity in readiness probe.
Verify Redis/cache connectivity in readiness probe.
Verify critical external service dependencies in readiness probe.
Use vendor's documented status endpoint, not invented URLs.
Return proper status codes: 200 for healthy, 503 for degraded.
Test health checks manually before wiring to orchestrator.
Configure liveness probe to /healthz only; readiness probe to /readyz.

antipattern.description: Configuring Kubernetes liveness probe to hit /readyz (which calls downstream dependencies).
antipattern.reason: If a downstream dependency is slow, the liveness probe fails, triggering container restarts. The pod restarts into the same degraded dependency, causing a restart loop that takes down the service even though the process itself is healthy.

antipattern.description: Using a single /health endpoint for both liveness and readiness without distinguishing what it checks.
antipattern.reason: Forces a trade-off: either risk cascade restarts (if it checks downstream) or provide no signal about dependency health (if it doesn't). Both outcomes are worse than the split pattern.

antipattern.description: Using invented or undocumented vendor health URLs like https://api.stripe.com/health in the readiness probe.
antipattern.reason: Undocumented URLs can return 404 at any time or not exist at all. The probe then always fails or produces false positives. Always verify health endpoint contracts from official vendor documentation.
